---
title: "Export tables"
subtitle: "`to_table()` and `FeatureAlignment.to_dataframe()` turn workflow objects into rows."
sidebar: start
---

## From object to table

Feature objects and alignments are the in-memory contract. Table helpers provide an interchange surface for modeling, inspection, and downstream joins.

```{python}
from natural_features.workflows import extract_features

result = extract_features(
    "The scene opens on a quiet room.",
    features=["text.tokenize", "language.surface"],
)
words = result.features["text.tokenize"]

table = result.to_table(include_metadata=False)
table.loc[:, ["output_name", "output_type", "onset_s", "time_s", "label", "feature", "value"]].head(8)
```

## What gets added

The table includes event timing, clock reference, labels, feature names, values,
object columns when present, and provenance columns copied from metadata.

```{python}
sorted(set(table.columns) & {"output_name", "output_type", "schema", "time_reference", "object_id", "feature", "value"})
```

## Export an alignment table

Alignment rows answer a different question: which source row maps to which target timeline row?

```{python}
aligned = result.align_to("text.tokenize", features="language.surface")
aligned.to_dataframe().loc[
    :,
    ["source_name", "source_row", "target_name", "target_index_start", "target_time_s"],
]
```

## Refuses to

- Table helpers require pandas through the storage/docs dependency path.
- Event-level arrays in `extra` must have compatible lengths.
- Alignment table export requires a temporal source and a timeline target.
- Wide cross-clock joins require an explicit target clock and exact join policy.

## Read next

[Media and optional backends](05-media-and-optional-backends.qmd)
